title: Quiz: Fermionic Algebra
description: Test your understanding of fermionic anticommutation and Wick's theorem

questions.question: What is the anticommutator {cᵢ, cⱼ} for i ≠ j?
questions.explanation: {cᵢ, cⱼ} = cᵢcⱼ + cⱼcᵢ = 0 for all i, j. Two annihilation operators always anticommute, giving zero.

questions.question: What is the contraction of cᵢc†ⱼ?
questions.explanation: The contraction is defined as cᵢc†ⱼ - :cᵢc†ⱼ:. Since cᵢc†ⱼ = δᵢⱼ - c†ⱼcᵢ and :cᵢc†ⱼ: = -c†ⱼcᵢ, the contraction is δᵢⱼ.

questions.question: What is :cᵢc†ⱼ: (normal ordered product)?
questions.explanation: Normal ordering puts c† to the left of c. Moving cᵢ past c†ⱼ (ignoring the δᵢⱼ contraction) gives a sign: :cᵢc†ⱼ: = -c†ⱼcᵢ.

questions.question: According to Wick's theorem, ⟨0|ABCD|0⟩ equals what?

options.text: Sum of all fully contracted terms

questions.explanation: When taking the vacuum expectation value, only fully contracted terms survive because ⟨0|:anything:|0⟩ = 0. So ⟨0|ABCD|0⟩ = sum of fully contracted terms.

questions.question: (c†ᵢ)² = 0 for fermionic operators.
questions.explanation: True. From {c†ᵢ, c†ᵢ} = 2(c†ᵢ)² = 0, we get (c†ᵢ)² = 0. This is the Pauli exclusion principle—you cannot create two fermions in the same state.

questions.question: What is the sign of c†₂c†₁c†₃|0⟩ relative to c†₁c†₂c†₃|0⟩?
questions.explanation: c†₂c†₁ = -c†₁c†₂ (one swap), so c†₂c†₁c†₃|0⟩ = -c†₁c†₂c†₃|0⟩. The relative sign is -1.

questions.question: In the particle-hole formalism, what creates an 'excitation' from the HF ground state?
questions.explanation: A single excitation c†ₐcᵢ removes an electron from occupied orbital i (creating a hole) and places it in virtual orbital a (creating a particle).

questions.question: Which contractions are non-zero? (Select all)
questions.explanation: Only the contraction cᵢc†ⱼ is non-zero, giving δᵢⱼ. All other elementary contractions (c†c, cc, c†c†) are zero.
